records.ety: [OF. communier, fr. L. communicare to communicate, fr. communis common. See Common, and cf. Communicate.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Communed (km*mūnd"); p. pr. & vb. n. Communing.]

defs: To converse together with sympathy and confidence; to interchange sentiments or feelings; to take counsel.
quotes: I would commune with you of such things That want no ear but yours.

defs: To receive the communion; to partake of the Eucharist or Lord's supper.
Communion; sympathetic intercourse or conversation between friends.
quotes: For days of happy commune dead.
In this struggle -- to use the technical words of the time -- of the “commune”, the general mass of the inhabitants, against the “prudhommes” or “wiser” few.

defs: A small territorial district in France under the government of a mayor and municipal council; also, the inhabitants, or the government, of such a district. See Arrondissement.

a group of people living together as an organized community and owning in common most or all of their property and possessions, and sharing work, income, and many other aspects of daily life. Such communities are often organized based on religious or idealistic principles, and they sometimes have unconventional lifestyles, practices, or moral codes.
